title: Incomplete path traversal validation in File.isValid(path:)
description: The path validation function File.isValid(path:) only checks for paths starting with '.' and containing '/..' or '../'. This can be bypassed using encoded or alternative traversal sequences. More critically, NSString.standardizingPath (used later to normalize) does NOT resolve symlinks, so a malicious symlink placed inside the files root (e.g. via upload) pointing to '/' would allow escaping the root directory. The function also does not check for null bytes in the path string. Additionally, paths like '..foo' or trailing '/..' without a slash are not caught by the current checks (though 'contains(/..)'  covers trailing '..'). The real issue is that paths are validated BEFORE normalization: File.isValid is called on the raw user input, then standardizingPath is applied. A path like '/foo/bar/.' passes isValid but could interact unexpectedly with path operations.
recommendation: 1. After normalization with standardizingPath, verify the resolved real path starts with the root path prefix (jail check). 2. Resolve symlinks on the real path and re-check the jail. 3. Reject null bytes in paths. 4. Use URL.resolvingSymlinksInPath() consistently before all filesystem operations and verify the result remains within rootPath.
